agent_output: {"executive_summary":"Decompose the courier platform monolith into 6 bounded services aligned with domain boundaries. The decomposition prioritizes deployment independence (the stated goal), respects the consistency requirements of payments, and isolates the high-throughput location tracking write path. An event-driven backbone connects services, with synchronous calls only where transactional consistency demands it. The 8-engineer team maps well to 3 two-person service teams plus a platform pair, avoiding the microservice-per-engineer trap.","architecture":{"components":[{"name":"Order Service","responsibility":"Order lifecycle: placement, state transitions (placed → assigned → picked-up → delivered → completed), order history queries. Owns the Order aggregate.","boundaries":"Owns orders table/schema. Emits OrderPlaced, OrderStatusChanged events. Does NOT own courier selection logic or payment execution."},{"name":"Courier Service","responsibility":"Courier profiles, availability, assignment algorithm, route optimization. Owns the Courier and Assignment aggregates.","boundaries":"Owns courier and assignment schemas. Consumes OrderPlaced to trigger assignment. Emits CourierAssigned, CourierEnRoute. Does NOT own raw location data storage."},{"name":"Location Service","responsibility":"Ingests high-frequency GPS updates (5,000 writes/sec peak), stores current and historical positions, exposes location queries and geofence events.","boundaries":"Owns location time-series data. Optimized for write throughput (append-only store or time-series DB). Emits LocationUpdated, GeofenceEntered events. Stateless ingest path, no business logic beyond geofencing."},{"name":"Payment Service","responsibility":"Payment authorization, capture, refunds, ledger entries. Enforces strict transactional consistency on all monetary operations.","boundaries":"Owns payment and ledger schemas. Consumes OrderCompleted to trigger capture. Emits PaymentCaptured, RefundIssued. Synchronous confirmation back to Order Service for critical paths. Isolated from high-throughput paths."},{"name":"Notification Service","responsibility":"Fan-out of customer and courier notifications across email, SMS, and push channels. Template management, delivery tracking, retry logic.","boundaries":"Owns notification templates and delivery log. Purely event-driven consumer—no upstream services depend on it synchronously. Consumes domain events from all other services."},{"name":"Customer Service","responsibility":"Customer accounts, profiles, authentication, preferences, saved addresses.","boundaries":"Owns customer schema. Exposes customer identity and profile APIs consumed by Order and Notification services. Low change velocity—stable contract."}],"interfaces":[{"from":"Order Service","to":"Courier Service","contract":"OrderPlaced event (async via message broker) containing orderId, pickup/dropoff coordinates, time constraints","coupling_type":"event (loose)"},{"from":"Courier Service","to":"Order Service","contract":"CourierAssigned event (async) containing orderId, courierId, ETA","coupling_type":"event (loose)"},{"from":"Order Service","to":"Payment Service","contract":"Synchronous RPC for payment authorization at order placement; OrderCompleted event (async) triggers capture","coupling_type":"sync for auth, async for capture"},{"from":"Payment Service","to":"Order Service","contract":"PaymentAuthorized / PaymentFailed synchronous response; PaymentCaptured / RefundIssued async events","coupling_type":"sync response + async events"},{"from":"Location Service","to":"Courier Service","contract":"LocationUpdated events (high-frequency, filtered); Courier Service queries Location Service for current position during assignment","coupling_type":"event (loose) + sync query"},{"from":"All Services","to":"Notification Service","contract":"Domain events (OrderPlaced, CourierAssigned, OrderDelivered, PaymentCaptured, etc.) consumed by Notification Service to trigger customer/courier comms","coupling_type":"event (loose, fire-and-forget)"},{"from":"Order Service","to":"Customer Service","contract":"Sync REST/gRPC call to validate customer identity and fetch profile at order placement","coupling_type":"sync query (low frequency)"}],"data_flow":"1. Customer places order → Order Service validates customer (sync call to Customer Service), authorizes payment (sync call to Payment Service), persists order, emits OrderPlaced. 2. Courier Service consumes OrderPlaced, runs assignment algorithm (queries Location Service for nearby couriers), assigns courier, emits CourierAssigned. 3. Courier app sends GPS pings → Location Service ingests at high throughput, emits LocationUpdated for geofence triggers. 4. Courier Service consumes location events to update ETAs and detect arrival. 5. On delivery confirmation → Order Service emits OrderCompleted → Payment Service captures funds → Notification Service sends receipt. All async events flow through a central message broker (e.g., Kafka or RabbitMQ).","patterns_used":[{"pattern":"Event-Driven Architecture","rationale":"Primary integration pattern. Decouples services temporally and allows independent deployment—directly serves the stated goal of reducing deployment coupling."},{"pattern":"Database-per-Service","rationale":"Each service owns its schema exclusively. Eliminates shared-database coupling that is the #1 killer of microservice independence."},{"pattern":"CQRS (Location Service only)","rationale":"Location Service separates the high-throughput write path (GPS ingest) from the read path (current position queries). Write-optimized store (time-series DB like TimescaleDB or InfluxDB) vs read-optimized materialized views."},{"pattern":"Strangler Fig Migration","rationale":"Recommended migration approach. Extract one service at a time from the monolith, starting with Location Service (clearest boundary, highest technical pressure), rather than a risky big-bang rewrite."},{"pattern":"Saga (Choreography-based)","rationale":"Order placement spans Order + Payment. Use a choreography saga: Order Service emits events, Payment Service responds. Avoids a central orchestrator and keeps coupling low for this team size."}]},"domain_model":{"bounded_contexts":[{"name":"Order Management","classification":"core","aggregates":["Order (root: orderId, states: placed/assigned/picked-up/delivered/completed/cancelled, line items, timestamps)","DeliveryWindow (value object: pickup time, dropoff deadline)"]},{"name":"Courier Operations","classification":"core","aggregates":["Courier (root: courierId, availability status, vehicle type, current zone)","Assignment (root: assignmentId, links orderId ↔ courierId, state: pending/accepted/in-progress/completed)"]},{"name":"Location Tracking","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["CourierPosition (root: courierId+timestamp, lat/lng, accuracy, speed — append-only)","Geofence (root: fenceId, polygon, trigger rules)"]},{"name":"Payments","classification":"core","aggregates":["Payment (root: paymentId, orderId, amount, state: authorized/captured/refunded, idempotency key)","LedgerEntry (value object: debit/credit, timestamp, reference)"]},{"name":"Notifications","classification":"generic","aggregates":["NotificationRequest (root: notificationId, channel, template, recipient, delivery status)"]},{"name":"Customer Accounts","classification":"generic","aggregates":["Customer (root: customerId, profile, auth credentials, preferences, saved addresses)"]}],"context_map":[{"upstream":"Order Management","downstream":"Courier Operations","relationship":"Customer-Supplier (Order Management publishes OrderPlaced; Courier Operations consumes and conforms to the order model)"},{"upstream":"Order Management","downstream":"Payments","relationship":"Customer-Supplier (Order Management drives payment lifecycle via events and sync auth calls)"},{"upstream":"Location Tracking","downstream":"Courier Operations","relationship":"Open Host Service (Location Tracking exposes a query API and event stream; Courier Operations consumes positions without dictating the location model)"},{"upstream":"Order Management","downstream":"Notifications","relationship":"Conformist (Notifications consumes order events as-is; no negotiation power, adapts to upstream schema)"},{"upstream":"Courier Operations","downstream":"Notifications","relationship":"Conformist"},{"upstream":"Payments","downstream":"Notifications","relationship":"Conformist"},{"upstream":"Customer Accounts","downstream":"Order Management","relationship":"Shared Kernel (customerId is a shared identity; minimal shared schema kept deliberately small)"}]},"trade_offs":[{"decision":"6 services for 8 engineers","gains":"Clear domain alignment, independent deployability, manageable cognitive load per team","sacrifices":"Higher operational overhead than a monolith; some services may feel small. Risk of one engineer becoming a single point of failure for a service.","alternatives_considered":["Fewer services (merge Courier + Location): rejected because Location's write-throughput profile is fundamentally different and would pollute Courier Service's simpler domain model","More services (split Order into Order + Delivery): rejected as premature for team size; can split later if order management complexity grows"]},{"decision":"Event-driven integration as default, sync only for payment auth","gains":"Loose coupling, independent deployability, natural audit trail via event log","sacrifices":"Eventual consistency across most service boundaries; debugging distributed event flows is harder than tracing monolith calls","alternatives_considered":["Synchronous REST everywhere: rejected because it recreates a distributed monolith","Full orchestration (Saga orchestrator): rejected as over-engineering for current team maturity and service count"]},{"decision":"CQRS for Location Service only","gains":"Isolates the 5,000 writes/sec hot path; can use a specialized time-series store without affecting other services","sacrifices":"Adds operational complexity for one service; team needs to learn time-series DB ops","alternatives_considered":["CQRS across all services: rejected as unnecessary complexity; other services have modest throughput","Single relational DB for location: rejected because 5K writes/sec with 10-second granularity favors append-optimized storage"]},{"decision":"Choreography sagas over orchestration","gains":"No central coordinator to maintain; each service reacts to events independently; simpler for 2-3 step workflows","sacrifices":"Harder to visualize end-to-end flow; compensating actions (e.g., payment rollback on assignment failure) must be implemented in each service","alternatives_considered":["Orchestration saga with a dedicated saga service: viable but adds operational overhead and a coordination bottleneck the team isn't ready for"]}],"flexibility_points":[{"area":"Notification channels","evolution_path":"Adding new channels (WhatsApp, in-app) requires only changes to the Notification Service. No upstream changes needed since it consumes generic domain events."},{"area":"Assignment algorithm","evolution_path":"Courier assignment logic is isolated in Courier Service. Can evolve from simple nearest-courier to ML-based optimization without touching Order or Location services."},{"area":"Location storage technology","evolution_path":"Location Service's internal storage is behind its API boundary. Can migrate from TimescaleDB to ClickHouse or a custom solution without affecting consumers."},{"area":"Payment provider","evolution_path":"Payment Service encapsulates provider integration (Stripe, Adyen, etc.). Swapping providers or adding fallback providers is contained within one service."}],"rigidity_points":[{"area":"Order state machine","reason":"Order states (placed → assigned → picked-up → delivered → completed) are referenced by multiple downstream services via events. Changing this state machine requires coordinated schema evolution across event consumers. Invest in a versioned event schema from day one."},{"area":"customerId as shared identity","reason":"Customer ID is the minimal shared kernel between Customer Accounts and Order Management. Changing the identity scheme (e.g., migrating to UUIDs) would ripple across all services."},{"area":"Payment authorization in the order placement critical path","reason":"This is the one synchronous cross-service call in the happy path. Payment Service downtime blocks order placement. This coupling is intentional—you cannot place an order without payment validation—but it means Payment Service has the strictest availability SLA."}],"risks":[{"risk":"Operational maturity gap: team has run a monolith for 3 years, now must operate 6 services, a message broker, and per-service databases","severity":"high","mitigation":"Adopt Strangler Fig—extract one service at a time over 3-6 months. Start with Location Service (clearest boundary). Invest in shared observability (distributed tracing, centralized logging) before extracting the second service. Consider a platform engineer role from the 8-person team."},{"risk":"Data consistency across Order and Payment services during failures (e.g., payment authorized but order creation fails)","severity":"high","mitigation":"Implement idempotency keys on all payment operations. Use the choreography saga with compensating actions (void authorization if order fails to persist). Add a reconciliation job that detects and resolves orphaned authorizations."},{"risk":"Location Service throughput: 5,000 writes/sec requires careful technology selection and load testing","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Use a time-series database purpose-built for this workload. Batch GPS writes where possible (micro-batching at 1-second intervals). Load test to 2x peak (10K writes/sec) before going live. The Location Service should be the first extracted to validate the architecture under load."},{"risk":"Event schema evolution causing consumer breakdowns","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Adopt a schema registry (e.g., Confluent Schema Registry or a lightweight JSON Schema validator). Use backward-compatible evolution rules: new fields are optional, removed fields are deprecated before removal. Version events explicitly."},{"risk":"Single point of failure per service with 8 engineers spread across 6 services","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Ensure at least 2 engineers have context on each service (pair programming, code review rotation). Keep the Customer and Notification services simple enough that any engineer can maintain them. Document runbooks per service."}],"implementation_guidance":{"recommended_approach":"Use the Strangler Fig pattern to incrementally extract services from the monolith. Recommended extraction order: (1) Location Service — highest technical pressure, clearest boundary, validates the event infrastructure. (2) Notification Service — pure consumer, low risk, builds team confidence with event-driven patterns. (3) Payment Service — strict consistency requirements benefit from isolation; extract once the team is comfortable with inter-service communication. (4) Courier Service — depends on Location Service being extracted first. (5) Customer Service — low change velocity, extract last or keep in the monolith if it isn't causing deployment coupling pain. (6) Order Service — the last piece of the monolith becomes the Order Service. Each extraction phase should take 4-6 weeks, with the full migration completing in approximately 6-9 months.","files_to_create_or_modify":["docs/adr/001-service-decomposition.md — Architecture Decision Record documenting this decomposition and rationale","docs/adr/002-event-driven-integration.md — ADR for choosing event-driven as default integration pattern","docs/adr/003-location-service-cqrs.md — ADR for CQRS and time-series DB choice in Location Service","docs/contracts/events/ — Directory of event schema definitions (JSON Schema or Avro) for all domain events","docs/runbooks/ — Per-service operational runbooks","infra/docker-compose.yml — Local development environment with message broker and per-service databases","services/location-service/ — First service to extract; start with GPS ingest endpoint and event publisher","services/notification-service/ — Second extraction; event consumer + channel adapters"],"patterns_to_follow":"Database-per-service (no shared schemas). Event-carried state transfer for cross-service data needs. API Gateway or BFF for client-facing aggregation. Circuit breaker on the synchronous Order→Payment call. Idempotency keys on all payment and order mutation operations. Correlation IDs on all requests and events for distributed tracing.","pitfalls_to_avoid":["Do NOT create a shared database—this is the most common microservice anti-pattern and would negate all deployment independence gains","Do NOT extract all services simultaneously—the Strangler Fig approach is critical for a team with monolith-only operational experience","Do NOT use synchronous calls between services except for the Payment authorization path—every unnecessary sync call recreates the distributed monolith","Do NOT skip investing in observability infrastructure (tracing, centralized logging, alerting) before extracting the second service—you will be flying blind","Do NOT let the Location Service events flood the main event bus—use a separate topic/channel with appropriate retention and consumer group isolation","Do NOT over-normalize the event payloads—include enough data in each event that consumers rarely need to call back to the source service (event-carried state transfer)"]},"escalations":[],"confidence":{"level":82,"high_confidence_areas":["Service boundary decomposition aligned with domain boundaries","Location Service isolation and CQRS rationale given 5K writes/sec","Payment Service strict consistency isolation","Strangler Fig migration strategy for team maturity level","Event-driven integration as the default coupling pattern"],"low_confidence_areas":["Exact message broker choice (Kafka vs RabbitMQ) — depends on team familiarity and operational preferences not specified","Whether Customer Service warrants extraction or should remain in the monolith — depends on actual deployment coupling pain","Saga compensation complexity — real-world edge cases in Order+Payment choreography may push toward orchestration"],"assumptions":["The 8-engineer team can dedicate at least one engineer to platform/infrastructure work during the migration","The existing monolith has a reasonably modular codebase where domain boundaries are somewhat visible in the code","The 5,000 writes/sec figure is validated and represents true peak, not average","The team has or can acquire basic container orchestration capability (Docker, Kubernetes or similar)","Budget exists for additional infrastructure (message broker, per-service databases, observability tooling)"]}}
agent_output_excerpt: {"executive_summary":"Decompose the courier platform monolith into 6 bounded services aligned with domain boundaries. The decomposition prioritizes deployment independence (the stated goal), respects the consistency requirements of payments, and isolates the high-throughput location tracking write path. An event-driven backbone connects services, with synchronous calls only where transactional consistency demands it. The 8-engineer team maps well to 3 two-person service teams plus a platform pai

logging, alerting) before extracting the second service—you will be flying blind","Do NOT let the Location Service events flood the main event bus—use a separate topic/channel with appropriate retention and consumer group isolation","Do NOT over-normalize the event payloads—include enough data in each event that consumers rarely need to call back to the source service (event-carried state transfer)"]},"escalations":[],"confidence":{"level":82,"high_confidence_areas":["Service boundary decomposition aligned with domain boundaries","Location Service isolation and CQRS rationale given 5K writes/sec","Payment Service strict consistency isolation","Strangler Fig migration strategy for team maturity level","Event-driven integration as the default coupling pattern"],"low_confidence_areas":["Exact message broker choice (Kafka vs RabbitMQ) — depends on team familiarity and operational preferences not specified","Whether Customer Service warrants extraction or should remain in the monolith — depends on actual deployment coupling pain","Saga compensation complexity — real-world edge cases in Order+Payment choreography may push toward orchestration"],"assumptions":["The 8-engineer team can dedicate at least one engineer to platform/infrastructure work during the migration","The existing monolith has a reasonably modular codebase where domain boundaries are somewhat visible in the code","The 5,000 writes/sec figure is validated and represents true peak, not average","The team has or can acquire basic container orchestration trace.result: {"executive_summary":"Decompose the courier platform monolith into 6 bounded services aligned with domain boundaries. The decomposition prioritizes deployment independence (the stated goal), respects the consistency requirements of payments, and isolates the high-throughput location tracking write path. An event-driven backbone connects services, with synchronous calls only where transactional consistency demands it. The 8-engineer team maps well to 3 two-person service teams plus a platform pair, avoiding the microservice-per-engineer trap.","architecture":{"components":[{"name":"Order Service","responsibility":"Order lifecycle: placement, state transitions (placed → assigned → picked-up → delivered → completed), order history queries. Owns the Order aggregate.","boundaries":"Owns orders table/schema. Emits OrderPlaced, OrderStatusChanged events. Does NOT own courier selection logic or payment execution."},{"name":"Courier Service","responsibility":"Courier profiles, availability, assignment algorithm, route optimization. Owns the Courier and Assignment aggregates.","boundaries":"Owns courier and assignment schemas. Consumes OrderPlaced to trigger assignment. Emits CourierAssigned, CourierEnRoute. Does NOT own raw location data storage."},{"name":"Location Service","responsibility":"Ingests high-frequency GPS updates (5,000 writes/sec peak), stores current and historical positions, exposes location queries and geofence events.","boundaries":"Owns location time-series data. Optimized for write throughput (append-only store or time-series DB). Emits LocationUpdated, GeofenceEntered events. Stateless ingest path, no business logic beyond geofencing."},{"name":"Payment Service","responsibility":"Payment authorization, capture, refunds, ledger entries. Enforces strict transactional consistency on all monetary operations.","boundaries":"Owns payment and ledger schemas. Consumes OrderCompleted to trigger capture. Emits PaymentCaptured, RefundIssued. Synchronous confirmation back to Order Service for critical paths. Isolated from high-throughput paths."},{"name":"Notification Service","responsibility":"Fan-out of customer and courier notifications across email, SMS, and push channels. Template management, delivery tracking, retry logic.","boundaries":"Owns notification templates and delivery log. Purely event-driven consumer—no upstream services depend on it synchronously. Consumes domain events from all other services."},{"name":"Customer Service","responsibility":"Customer accounts, profiles, authentication, preferences, saved addresses.","boundaries":"Owns customer schema. Exposes customer identity and profile APIs consumed by Order and Notification services. Low change velocity—stable contract."}],"interfaces":[{"from":"Order Service","to":"Courier Service","contract":"OrderPlaced event (async via message broker) containing orderId, pickup/dropoff coordinates, time constraints","coupling_type":"event (loose)"},{"from":"Courier Service","to":"Order Service","contract":"CourierAssigned event (async) containing orderId, courierId, ETA","coupling_type":"event (loose)"},{"from":"Order Service","to":"Payment Service","contract":"Synchronous RPC for payment authorization at order placement; OrderCompleted event (async) triggers capture","coupling_type":"sync for auth, async for capture"},{"from":"Payment Service","to":"Order Service","contract":"PaymentAuthorized / PaymentFailed synchronous response; PaymentCaptured / RefundIssued async events","coupling_type":"sync response + async events"},{"from":"Location Service","to":"Courier Service","contract":"LocationUpdated events (high-frequency, filtered); Courier Service queries Location Service for current position during assignment","coupling_type":"event (loose) + sync query"},{"from":"All Services","to":"Notification Service","contract":"Domain events (OrderPlaced, CourierAssigned, OrderDelivered, PaymentCaptured, etc.) consumed by Notification Service to trigger customer/courier comms","coupling_type":"event (loose, fire-and-forget)"},{"from":"Order Service","to":"Customer Service","contract":"Sync REST/gRPC call to validate customer identity and fetch profile at order placement","coupling_type":"sync query (low frequency)"}],"data_flow":"1. Customer places order → Order Service validates customer (sync call to Customer Service), authorizes payment (sync call to Payment Service), persists order, emits OrderPlaced. 2. Courier Service consumes OrderPlaced, runs assignment algorithm (queries Location Service for nearby couriers), assigns courier, emits CourierAssigned. 3. Courier app sends GPS pings → Location Service ingests at high throughput, emits LocationUpdated for geofence triggers. 4. Courier Service consumes location events to update ETAs and detect arrival. 5. On delivery confirmation → Order Service emits OrderCompleted → Payment Service captures funds → Notification Service sends receipt. All async events flow through a central message broker (e.g., Kafka or RabbitMQ).","patterns_used":[{"pattern":"Event-Driven Architecture","rationale":"Primary integration pattern. Decouples services temporally and allows independent deployment—directly serves the stated goal of reducing deployment coupling."},{"pattern":"Database-per-Service","rationale":"Each service owns its schema exclusively. Eliminates shared-database coupling that is the #1 killer of microservice independence."},{"pattern":"CQRS (Location Service only)","rationale":"Location Service separates the high-throughput write path (GPS ingest) from the read path (current position queries). Write-optimized store (time-series DB like TimescaleDB or InfluxDB) vs read-optimized materialized views."},{"pattern":"Strangler Fig Migration","rationale":"Recommended migration approach. Extract one service at a time from the monolith, starting with Location Service (clearest boundary, highest technical pressure), rather than a risky big-bang rewrite."},{"pattern":"Saga (Choreography-based)","rationale":"Order placement spans Order + Payment. Use a choreography saga: Order Service emits events, Payment Service responds. Avoids a central orchestrator and keeps coupling low for this team size."}]},"domain_model":{"bounded_contexts":[{"name":"Order Management","classification":"core","aggregates":["Order (root: orderId, states: placed/assigned/picked-up/delivered/completed/cancelled, line items, timestamps)","DeliveryWindow (value object: pickup time, dropoff deadline)"]},{"name":"Courier Operations","classification":"core","aggregates":["Courier (root: courierId, availability status, vehicle type, current zone)","Assignment (root: assignmentId, links orderId ↔ courierId, state: pending/accepted/in-progress/completed)"]},{"name":"Location Tracking","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["CourierPosition (root: courierId+timestamp, lat/lng, accuracy, speed — append-only)","Geofence (root: fenceId, polygon, trigger rules)"]},{"name":"Payments","classification":"core","aggregates":["Payment (root: paymentId, orderId, amount, state: authorized/captured/refunded, idempotency key)","LedgerEntry (value object: debit/credit, timestamp, reference)"]},{"name":"Notifications","classification":"generic","aggregates":["NotificationRequest (root: notificationId, channel, template, recipient, delivery status)"]},{"name":"Customer Accounts","classification":"generic","aggregates":["Customer (root: customerId, profile, auth credentials, preferences, saved addresses)"]}],"context_map":[{"upstream":"Order Management","downstream":"Courier Operations","relationship":"Customer-Supplier (Order Management publishes OrderPlaced; Courier Operations consumes and conforms to the order model)"},{"upstream":"Order Management","downstream":"Payments","relationship":"Customer-Supplier (Order Management drives payment lifecycle via events and sync auth calls)"},{"upstream":"Location Tracking","downstream":"Courier Operations","relationship":"Open Host Service (Location Tracking exposes a query API and event stream; Courier Operations consumes positions without dictating the location model)"},{"upstream":"Order Management","downstream":"Notifications","relationship":"Conformist (Notifications consumes order events as-is; no negotiation power, adapts to upstream schema)"},{"upstream":"Courier Operations","downstream":"Notifications","relationship":"Conformist"},{"upstream":"Payments","downstream":"Notifications","relationship":"Conformist"},{"upstream":"Customer Accounts","downstream":"Order Management","relationship":"Shared Kernel (customerId is a shared identity; minimal shared schema kept deliberately small)"}]},"trade_offs":[{"decision":"6 services for 8 engineers","gains":"Clear domain alignment, independent deployability, manageable cognitive load per team","sacrifices":"Higher operational overhead than a monolith; some services may feel small. Risk of one engineer becoming a single point of failure for a service.","alternatives_considered":["Fewer services (merge Courier + Location): rejected because Location's write-throughput profile is fundamentally different and would pollute Courier Service's simpler domain model","More services (split Order into Order + Delivery): rejected as premature for team size; can split later if order management complexity grows"]},{"decision":"Event-driven integration as default, sync only for payment auth","gains":"Loose coupling, independent deployability, natural audit trail via event log","sacrifices":"Eventual consistency across most service boundaries; debugging distributed event flows is harder than tracing monolith calls","alternatives_considered":["Synchronous REST everywhere: rejected because it recreates a distributed monolith","Full orchestration (Saga orchestrator): rejected as over-engineering for current team maturity and service count"]},{"decision":"CQRS for Location Service only","gains":"Isolates the 5,000 writes/sec hot path; can use a specialized time-series store without affecting other services","sacrifices":"Adds operational complexity for one service; team needs to learn time-series DB ops","alternatives_considered":["CQRS across all services: rejected as unnecessary complexity; other services have modest throughput","Single relational DB for location: rejected because 5K writes/sec with 10-second granularity favors append-optimized storage"]},{"decision":"Choreography sagas over orchestration","gains":"No central coordinator to maintain; each service reacts to events independently; simpler for 2-3 step workflows","sacrifices":"Harder to visualize end-to-end flow; compensating actions (e.g., payment rollback on assignment failure) must be implemented in each service","alternatives_considered":["Orchestration saga with a dedicated saga service: viable but adds operational overhead and a coordination bottleneck the team isn't ready for"]}],"flexibility_points":[{"area":"Notification channels","evolution_path":"Adding new channels (WhatsApp, in-app) requires only changes to the Notification Service. No upstream changes needed since it consumes generic domain events."},{"area":"Assignment algorithm","evolution_path":"Courier assignment logic is isolated in Courier Service. Can evolve from simple nearest-courier to ML-based optimization without touching Order or Location services."},{"area":"Location storage technology","evolution_path":"Location Service's internal storage is behind its API boundary. Can migrate from TimescaleDB to ClickHouse or a custom solution without affecting consumers."},{"area":"Payment provider","evolution_path":"Payment Service encapsulates provider integration (Stripe, Adyen, etc.). Swapping providers or adding fallback providers is contained within one service."}],"rigidity_points":[{"area":"Order state machine","reason":"Order states (placed → assigned → picked-up → delivered → completed) are referenced by multiple downstream services via events. Changing this state machine requires coordinated schema evolution across event consumers. Invest in a versioned event schema from day one."},{"area":"customerId as shared identity","reason":"Customer ID is the minimal shared kernel between Customer Accounts and Order Management. Changing the identity scheme (e.g., migrating to UUIDs) would ripple across all services."},{"area":"Payment authorization in the order placement critical path","reason":"This is the one synchronous cross-service call in the happy path. Payment Service downtime blocks order placement. This coupling is intentional—you cannot place an order without payment validation—but it means Payment Service has the strictest availability SLA."}],"risks":[{"risk":"Operational maturity gap: team has run a monolith for 3 years, now must operate 6 services, a message broker, and per-service databases","severity":"high","mitigation":"Adopt Strangler Fig—extract one service at a time over 3-6 months. Start with Location Service (clearest boundary). Invest in shared observability (distributed tracing, centralized logging) before extracting the second service. Consider a platform engineer role from the 8-person team."},{"risk":"Data consistency across Order and Payment services during failures (e.g., payment authorized but order creation fails)","severity":"high","mitigation":"Implement idempotency keys on all payment operations. Use the choreography saga with compensating actions (void authorization if order fails to persist). Add a reconciliation job that detects and resolves orphaned authorizations."},{"risk":"Location Service throughput: 5,000 writes/sec requires careful technology selection and load testing","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Use a time-series database purpose-built for this workload. Batch GPS writes where possible (micro-batching at 1-second intervals). Load test to 2x peak (10K writes/sec) before going live. The Location Service should be the first extracted to validate the architecture under load."},{"risk":"Event schema evolution causing consumer breakdowns","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Adopt a schema registry (e.g., Confluent Schema Registry or a lightweight JSON Schema validator). Use backward-compatible evolution rules: new fields are optional, removed fields are deprecated before removal. Version events explicitly."},{"risk":"Single point of failure per service with 8 engineers spread across 6 services","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Ensure at least 2 engineers have context on each service (pair programming, code review rotation). Keep the Customer and Notification services simple enough that any engineer can maintain them. Document runbooks per service."}],"implementation_guidance":{"recommended_approach":"Use the Strangler Fig pattern to incrementally extract services from the monolith. Recommended extraction order: (1) Location Service — highest technical pressure, clearest boundary, validates the event infrastructure. (2) Notification Service — pure consumer, low risk, builds team confidence with event-driven patterns. (3) Payment Service — strict consistency requirements benefit from isolation; extract once the team is comfortable with inter-service communication. (4) Courier Service — depends on Location Service being extracted first. (5) Customer Service — low change velocity, extract last or keep in the monolith if it isn't causing deployment coupling pain. (6) Order Service — the last piece of the monolith becomes the Order Service. Each extraction phase should take 4-6 weeks, with the full migration completing in approximately 6-9 months.","files_to_create_or_modify":["docs/adr/001-service-decomposition.md — Architecture Decision Record documenting this decomposition and rationale","docs/adr/002-event-driven-integration.md — ADR for choosing event-driven as default integration pattern","docs/adr/003-location-service-cqrs.md — ADR for CQRS and time-series DB choice in Location Service","docs/contracts/events/ — Directory of event schema definitions (JSON Schema or Avro) for all domain events","docs/runbooks/ — Per-service operational runbooks","infra/docker-compose.yml — Local development environment with message broker and per-service databases","services/location-service/ — First service to extract; start with GPS ingest endpoint and event publisher","services/notification-service/ — Second extraction; event consumer + channel adapters"],"patterns_to_follow":"Database-per-service (no shared schemas). Event-carried state transfer for cross-service data needs. API Gateway or BFF for client-facing aggregation. Circuit breaker on the synchronous Order→Payment call. Idempotency keys on all payment and order mutation operations. Correlation IDs on all requests and events for distributed tracing.","pitfalls_to_avoid":["Do NOT create a shared database—this is the most common microservice anti-pattern and would negate all deployment independence gains","Do NOT extract all services simultaneously—the Strangler Fig approach is critical for a team with monolith-only operational experience","Do NOT use synchronous calls between services except for the Payment authorization path—every unnecessary sync call recreates the distributed monolith","Do NOT skip investing in observability infrastructure (tracing, centralized logging, alerting) before extracting the second service—you will be flying blind","Do NOT let the Location Service events flood the main event bus—use a separate topic/channel with appropriate retention and consumer group isolation","Do NOT over-normalize the event payloads—include enough data in each event that consumers rarely need to call back to the source service (event-carried state transfer)"]},"escalations":[],"confidence":{"level":82,"high_confidence_areas":["Service boundary decomposition aligned with domain boundaries","Location Service isolation and CQRS rationale given 5K writes/sec","Payment Service strict consistency isolation","Strangler Fig migration strategy for team maturity level","Event-driven integration as the default coupling pattern"],"low_confidence_areas":["Exact message broker choice (Kafka vs RabbitMQ) — depends on team familiarity and operational preferences not specified","Whether Customer Service warrants extraction or should remain in the monolith — depends on actual deployment coupling pain","Saga compensation complexity — real-world edge cases in Order+Payment choreography may push toward orchestration"],"assumptions":["The 8-engineer team can dedicate at least one engineer to platform/infrastructure work during the migration","The existing monolith has a reasonably modular codebase where domain boundaries are somewhat visible in the code","The 5,000 writes/sec figure is validated and represents true peak, not average","The team has or can acquire basic container orchestration capability (Docker, Kubernetes or similar)","Budget exists for additional infrastructure (message broker, per-service databases, observability tooling)"]}}
